title: Properties, structure, and function of biological macromolecules
description: Test your knowledge of the properties, structure, and function of biological macromolecules
topic_path: Properties, structure, and function of biological macromolecules
Properties, structure, and function of biological macromolecules

assessment_items.question_content:
A researcher wants to identify a newly isolated DNA strand. The first step in identifying the DNA strand is to measure its length. To do this, the researcher wants to develop a fluorescent tag that emits light once it binds to both ends of the strand.

**Which of the following best describes how the researcher should develop a fluorescent tag with an ability to bind to both ends of the strand?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The tag should have an affinity to bind only free $3’$ hydroxyl ($\text{--OH}$) and $5’$ phosphate ($\text{--PO}_4$) functional groups.
The tag should have an affinity to bind only free amino ($\text{--NH}_2$) and carboxyl ($\text{--COOH}$) functional groups.
The tag should have an affinity to bind only adenine (A) and thymine (T) nitrogenous bases.
The tag should have an affinity to bind only guanine (G) and cytosine (C) nitrogenous bases.

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

*Image credit: Adapted from Public Domain*

In the image above, a thymine nucleotide and cytosine nucleotide are connected together as a dinucleotide. A single guanine nucleotide is also shown. 

**Which of the following best predicts how the free guanine nucleotide will attach to the dinucleotide?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: The diagram shows 3 nucleotides. The first nucleotide is thymine as indicated by a T attached to the first carbon in ribose. The 5 prime carbon is attached to an oxygen of a phosphate group. The 3 prime carbon of the thymine is connected to the second nucleotide by a bond between an oxygen of the phosphate group from the 5 prime carbon of the second nucleotide. The second nucleotide is cytosine as indicated by a C on the first carbon of ribose. The 3 prime carbon of the cytosine nucleotide is attached to an OH. The third nucleotide in the diagram is guanine as indicated by a G attached to the first carbon in ribose. The guanine nucleotide is not connected to any other nucleotides. The 5 prime carbon is connected to a phosphate molecule by an oxygen.

choices.content: Through dehydration synthesis, the guanine nucleotide will form a covalent bond at the $3’$ hydroxyl ($\text{--OH}$) of the cytosine nucleotide.
Through dehydration synthesis, the guanine nucleotide will form a covalent bond at the $3’$ hydroxyl ($\text{--OH}$) of the thymine nucleotide.
Through hydrolysis, the guanine nucleotide will form a covalent bond at the the $3’$ hydroxyl ($\text{--OH}$) of the cytosine nucleotide.
Through hydrolysis, the guanine nucleotide will form a covalent bond at the the $3’$ hydroxyl ($\text{--OH}$) of the thymine nucleotide.

assessment_items.question_content:
In order to develop antibiotics, scientists need to understand how bacteria grow, divide, and spread during an infection. An essential part of bacterial growth is RNA synthesis because RNA must be synthesized before proteins can be assembled. If RNA synthesis is blocked, bacteria will not be able to survive and reproduce. 

**Which of the following questions will best direct an investigation on how an antibiotic that inhibits RNA synthesis in bacteria can be developed?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Which chemical compounds block the $3’$ end of a growing RNA strand?
Which proteins are used to undergo cell division through binary fission?
Which chemical compounds add a hydroxyl ($\text{--OH}$) group to the growing RNA strand?
Which chemical compounds block the $5’$ end of the growing RNA strand?

assessment_items.question_content:
In the early 1950’s, a team of researchers led by Erwin Chargaff conducted a series of experiments related to deciphering the chemical composition of DNA. In one experiment, Chargaff’s team examined samples of DNA from different species of sea urchins. The results of this experiment are summarized in the table below.


[[☃ image 1]]

*Chargaff E, Lipshitz R, Green C (1952). "Composition of the deoxypentose nucleic acids of four genera of sea-urchin" (PDF). J Biol Chem. 195 (1): 155–160. PMID 14938364.*

**Which of the following results from the experiment is most consistent with the base-pairing rules of DNA?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A chart showing the molar ratios of 4 different species of sea urchins. The chart has 4 columns labeled with the different species of sea urchins. Column 1 is Psammechinus miliaris, Column 2 is Paracentrotus lividus, Column 3 is Echinocardium cordatum, and Column 4 is Arabica lixula. There are 5 rows of molar ratios. Row 1 is Adenine to guanine, row 2 is thymine to cytosine, row 3 is adenine to thymine, row 4 is guanine to cytosine and row 5 is purines to pyrimidines. The molar ratio values for Psammechinus are 1.83 for adenine to guanine, 1.80 for thymine to cytosine, 1.02 for adenine to thymine, 1.00 for guanine to cytosine, and 1.01 for purine to pyrimidines. The molar ratio values for Paracentrotus lividus are 1.85 for adenine to guanine, 1.85 for thymine to cytosine, 1.02 for adenine to thymine, 1.02 for guanine to cytosine, and 1.02 for purines and pyrimidines. The molar ratio values for Echinocardium cordatum are 1.93 for adenine to guanine, 1.79 for thymine to cytosine, 1.02 for adenine to thymine, 0.95 for guanine to cytosine and 1.00 for purines to pyrimidines. The molar ratio values for Arabica lixula are 1.64 for adenine to guanine, 1.58 for thymine to cytosine, 1.02 for adenine to thymine, 1.00 for guanine to cytosine and 1.01 for purines to pyrimidines.

choices.content: The percentage of adenine nucleotides is roughly equal to the percentage of thymine nucleotides across all species in the experiment.
The percentage of thymine nucleotides is roughly equal to the percentage of cytosine nucleotides across all species in the experiment.
The average molar ratio of guanine to cytosine is less than the average molar ratio of adenine to guanine across all species in the experiment.
The average molar ratio of adenine to guanine is greater than the average molar ratio of thymine to cytosine across all species in the experiment.

assessment_items.question_content:
In order to develop antibiotics, scientists need to understand how bacteria grow and reproduce. One factor that is essential for bacterial growth and reproduction is protein synthesis. By understanding how bacteria synthesize proteins, scientists can then attempt to develop antibiotics that block bacterial protein synthesis during an infection.

**Which of the following questions will best direct an investigation on how to develop an antibiotic that inhibits protein synthesis in bacteria?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: How can the carboxyl ($\text{--COOH}$) terminus of a growing polypeptide chain be blocked?
How can the amino ($\text{--NH}_2$) terminus of a growing polypeptide chain be blocked?
How can the $3’$ end of the growing polypeptide chain be blocked?
How can the $5’$ end of the growing polypeptide chain be blocked?

assessment_items.question_content:
The image below depicts how the bacterial protein barnase undergoes modifications that involve changing its conformation, or shape. In order to function in the cell, barnase has to be folded from a nonfunctional conformation into a functional conformation.


[[☃ image 1]]

*Image credit: By Christopher Rowley [CC BY-SA 4.0], from Wikimedia Commons*

**Based on the information above, which of the following best describes the functional conformation of barnase?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: An image of a thin green squiggly line representing the bacterial protein barnase. An arrow points to another image of the green squiggly line connected to 3 thick red coiled ribbons and 3 thick yellow arrows attached to the green line in different locations throughout the bacterial protein barnase. A terminal yellow arrow is attached at one end.

choices.content: The functional conformation of barnase has a tertiary structure because it consists of a polypeptide chain folded into a three-dimensional shape.
The functional conformation of barnase has a primary structure because it consists of a linear chain of amino acids.
The functional conformation of barnase has a secondary structure because it consists of a single alpha-helix.
The functional conformation of barnase has a quaternary structure because it consists of interactions between multiple polypeptide units.

assessment_items.question_content:
In order to develop antibiotics, scientists need to understand how bacteria grow and reproduce. One factor that is essential for bacterial growth and reproduction is protein synthesis. By understanding how bacteria synthesize proteins, scientists can then attempt to develop antibiotics that block bacterial protein synthesis during an infection.

**Which of the following questions will best direct an investigation on how to develop an antibiotic that inhibits protein synthesis in bacteria?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: How can the formation of covalent bonds between amino acids be blocked?
How can the formation of hydrogen bonds between amino acids be blocked?
How can the hydroxyl end of the growing polypeptide chain be blocked?
How can the phosphate end of the growing polypeptide chain be blocked?

assessment_items.question_content:
Most species of shark are strict carnivores. However, bonnethead sharks (*Sphyrna tiburo*) have been observed consuming seagrass within their natural habitat. Like other plants, seagrasses contain cellulose within their cell walls. 

**Based on the information above, which of the following best supports the claim that bonnethead sharks are capable of hydrolyzing cellulose during digestion?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Cellulose is consumed as a linear polysaccharide and is broken down into glucose monosaccharides during digestion.
Cellulose is consumed as a linear polysaccharide and is converted into branched glucose monosaccharides during digestion.
Cellulose is consumed as a linear polysaccharide and remains as a linear polysaccharide during digestion.
Cellulose is consumed as glucose monosaccharides and is converted into a linear polysaccharide during digestion.

assessment_items.question_content:
In the cell membrane, carbohydrate chains can be covalently linked to membrane proteins to form glycoproteins. They can also be covalently linked to membrane phospholipids to form glycolipids. These two cell membrane structures are shown in the image below. 


[[☃ image 1]]

During an investigation, researchers discover a new toxin that enters a cell after binding to a carbohydrate on the cell membrane.  

**Which of the following best describes how the toxin attaches to the cell membrane?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: A diagram of a phospholipid bilayer that has a protein in the bilayer that extends through the entire width of the bilayer. Attached to the structure is a Y shaped structure of 7 hexagons that is labeled carbohydrate chains. To the left of the protein with the carbohydrate chain attached is the label glycoprotein. Attached to one phospholipid is a branched structure with 12 hexagons that is labeled carbohydrate chains. To the right of the carbohydrate chain attached to the phospholipid is the label glycolipid.

choices.content: The toxin attaches to specific monosaccharides on the cell membrane.
The toxin attaches to specific amino acids on the cell membrane.
The toxin attaches to specific phospholipids on the cell membrane.
The toxin attaches to specific fatty acids on the cell membrane.

assessment_items.question_content:
Glycogen is a highly branched macromolecule usually stored in liver and muscle cells. If blood glucose levels decrease, glycogen breaks down to release glucose in a process called glycogenolysis. In this process, the glycogen polysaccharide goes through hydrolysis and breaks down into glucose monosaccharides.  

**If a toxin were to inhibit glycogenolysis, which of the following statements best predicts a consequence of this inhibition?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The covalent bonds between individual glucose monomers will not be cleaved.
The covalent bonds within an individual glucose monomer will not be cleaved.
In response to the detection of low glucose levels, more glucose will be released from stored glycogen.
In response to the detection of low glucose levels, glycogen will continuously be broken down to release glucose.
